section.title: Woe to the Complacent
section.sectionRationale: Amos turns from the funeral song of chapter five to a scorching woe on the elite of Samaria who feel utterly secure. He paints their luxury in vivid detail, ivory beds, choice meat, improvised music, bowls of wine, finest oils, and then drives home the fatal charge: they are not grieved over the ruin of Joseph. Complacency in the face of a dying nation is itself the sin, and it will send them into exile at the head of the column.
section.thesis: The comfortable self-confidence of those at ease in Zion, feasting while the nation crumbles and caring nothing for its ruin, is not innocence but guilt; God abhors such pride and will hand the secure city over to judgment.
section.frameNote: Three movements: the woe on the complacent who think themselves safe; the luxury of those who will not mourn; and the LORD's oath against Jacob's pride, ending in a nation raised up to oppress them.

units.label: Woe to those at ease
units.note: false security in Zion and Samaria

verses.text: {{hoy|Woe}} to those {{shaanan|at ease}} in Zion and those secure on Mount Samaria, the distinguished ones of the foremost nation, to whom the house of Israel comes.
Cross over to Calneh and see; go from there to the great Hamath; then go down to Gath of the Philistines. Are you better than these kingdoms? Is their territory larger than yours?
You dismiss the day of calamity and bring near a reign of violence.

units.label: The luxury that will not mourn
units.note: ivory beds while Joseph is ruined

verses.text: You lie on beds inlaid with ivory, and lounge upon your couches. You dine on lambs from the flock and calves from the stall.
You improvise songs on the harp like David and invent your own musical instruments.
You drink wine by the bowlful and anoint yourselves with the finest oils, but you fail to grieve over the {{sheber|ruin}} of Joseph.
Therefore, you will now go into exile as the first of the captives, and your feasting and lounging will come to an end.

units.label: The LORD abhors Jacob's pride
units.note: the sworn oath and the nation raised up

verses.text: The Lord GOD has sworn by Himself, the LORD, the God of Hosts, has declared: “I abhor Jacob’s pride and detest his citadels, so I will deliver up the city and everything in it.”
And if there are ten men left in one house, they too will die.

And when the relative who is to burn the bodies picks them up to remove them from the house, he will call to one inside, “Is anyone else with you?” “None,” that person will answer. “Silence,” the relative will retort, “for the name of the LORD must not be invoked.”
For the LORD gives a command: “The great house will be smashed to pieces, and the small house to rubble.”
“Do horses gallop on the cliffs? Does one plow the sea with oxen? But you have turned {{mishpat|justice}} into poison and the fruit of {{tsedaqah|righteousness}} into wormwood,
you who rejoice in Lo-debar and say, ‘Did we not take Karnaim by our own strength?’
For behold, I will raise up a nation against you, O house of Israel,” declares the LORD, the God of Hosts, “and they will oppress you from Lebo-hamath to the Brook of the Arabah.”

hoy.gloss: An exclamation of woe or alas, the cry raised over the dead at a funeral. Amos hurls it at the living elite of Samaria, pronouncing them as good as buried while they still lounge in luxury. The same word opens the woe on those who long for the Day of the LORD in chapter five; here it falls on those who feel most secure, turning their false peace into a lament.

shaanan.gloss: At ease, secure, untroubled, carefree. The word can describe a healthy peace, but here it names a deadly complacency: a settled confidence that all is well when disaster is at the door. Israel's leaders were relaxed and self-assured, dismissing the day of calamity, and it is precisely this unshaken ease, blind to the nation's ruin, that Amos exposes as sin.

sheber.gloss: A breaking, fracture, shattering, ruin. Joseph, standing for the northern kingdom, is being broken to pieces, yet the elite feast on without a pang of grief. The heart of the indictment is not merely their luxury but their failure to be grieved over the sheber of Joseph; they are unmoved by the very collapse God is bringing, and callousness toward it is itself guilt.

mishpat.gloss: Justice, judgment, the right ordering of society especially toward the vulnerable. As in chapter five, Israel has perverted it, here turning mishpat into poison. What should have brought life and protection has been made toxic in the hands of the powerful, a bitter reversal that God will not overlook.

tsedaqah.gloss: Righteousness, right conduct in relationship with God and neighbor. The fruit of righteousness, meant to nourish the community, has been turned into wormwood, a bitter and poisonous plant. Paired again with mishpat, it shows a people who have inverted God's own character, boasting in their own strength while the moral order rots.

basic.context: Amos rounds on the wealthy, self-satisfied leaders of Israel who feel completely safe. He describes their extravagant lifestyle: ivory beds, the best meat, casual music-making, wine drunk by the bowlful, expensive oils. Yet the deadly thing is what they do not do: they are not grieved over the ruin of their own nation. Because they feel secure and care nothing for the coming collapse, they will be the first to be marched off into exile. God swears He detests their pride and will hand the city over.
basic.hermeneutics: This is a woe oracle, a funeral cry pronounced over people who think they are perfectly safe. The point is not that comfort or music or food are wrong in themselves, but that comfort which numbs us to the suffering and coming judgment around us is a grave sin. Complacency is not neutral; it is guilt.
basic.originalLanguages: The chapter opens with hoy, woe, the wail raised over the dead, aimed at those who are shaanan, at ease and untroubled. Their crime is that they are not grieved over the sheber, the ruin, of Joseph. And once more they have turned mishpat, justice, into poison and the fruit of tsedaqah, righteousness, into wormwood.
basic.typology: The leaders feast blindly while ruin approaches, a picture answered in reverse by Christ, the true King who wept over a city facing judgment and gave up all comfort to save it. The complacent rich who ignore the day of calamity anticipate the man in Jesus' parable who stored up ease and lost his soul.
basic.christ: Where Israel's rulers lounged in luxury and would not grieve over Joseph's ruin, Jesus is the King who wept over Jerusalem, had nowhere to lay His head, and entered the nation's calamity to bear it Himself. The woe that falls on the complacent is the very judgment Christ absorbed at the cross, so that those who mourn their sin and take refuge in Him find mercy instead of exile.
basic.god: God is not indifferent to how the comfortable treat the collapsing world around them. He swears by Himself that He abhors self-exalting pride and will not let secure sinners feast their way past judgment. Yet the God who tears down the proud citadel is the same God who, in Christ, enters the ruin to rescue.
basic.crossReferences: Luke 6:24-25 pronounces woe on the rich and full who laugh now. Luke 12:16-21 tells of the rich fool at ease. James 5:1-6 warns the self-indulgent rich who ignored the cries of the poor. Luke 19:41-44 shows Christ weeping over a city facing judgment.
basic.oneStory: The complacent feast while the nation dies, and God will not let it stand. The true answer comes in Jesus, the King who refused comfort, wept over the doomed city, and took the woe of judgment upon Himself, so that those who mourn their sin can be spared.

context.literary: The chapter is bound together by two woe oracles and vivid catalogs of luxury. It moves from the false security of the elite, to a portrait of self-indulgent feasting, to God's solemn oath against Jacob's pride and the announcement of an unnamed nation raised up to oppress Israel. The rhetorical sting is the sudden turn in verse six: after listing every pleasure, Amos names the one thing missing, grief over the ruin of Joseph.
context.historical: This was the prosperous mid-eighth century under Jeroboam II, when Samaria's upper class enjoyed real wealth. Ivory inlays have been recovered from Samaria's ruins, confirming the picture. Calneh, Hamath, and Gath were cities that had fallen or been humbled, a warning that Israel was no safer. Lo-debar and Karnaim were towns east of the Jordan recently retaken; the leaders boasted in these small victories, ironic because Lo-debar sounds like nothing and Karnaim means horns, a symbol of strength.
context.canonical: Amos 6 stands in a long line of Scripture that indicts self-indulgent security blind to the suffering of others, developed in the prophets and echoed sharply by Jesus and James. The unnamed nation raised up to oppress Israel from Lebo-hamath to the Arabah points to Assyria, the instrument of the exile Amos keeps foretelling.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To shatter the false security of Israel's ruling class, to expose their luxury as guilt because it coexists with indifference to the nation's ruin, and to announce that their pride will be answered by exile at the hands of a nation God Himself will raise up.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The catalog of luxury describes a specific corrupt elite; it is not a blanket condemnation of food, music, or rest. The abiding principle it prescribes, by way of warning, is that comfort must never make us callous, and that those who feel most secure apart from God are in the gravest danger.

debates.question: Is Amos condemning wealth and pleasure themselves, or the complacency and injustice bound up with them?

views.label: The target is callous complacency, not comfort as such
views.case: The decisive charge in verse six is that they were not grieved over the ruin of Joseph; the luxury is condemned because it anesthetized them to the nation's collapse and coexisted with turning justice into poison, not because comfort is inherently sinful.

views.label: The target includes the self-indulgent excess itself
views.case: The sheer piling up of ivory, choice meat, bowls of wine, and finest oils suggests a decadent excess that God abhors alongside their pride; the luxury is not merely incidental but part of the arrogance He detests.

debates.bearing: Either way, the fatal issue is a heart at ease before God and unmoved by ruin and injustice. Scripture does not forbid rest or provision, but it warns that comfort which deadens compassion and dismisses the day of calamity is deadly.

study.originalLanguages: The oracle opens with hoy, the funeral cry of woe, pronounced over those who are shaanan, at ease, secure, carefree, a settled complacency that dismisses the day of calamity. Amos catalogs their pleasures and then lands the blow: they are not grieved over the sheber, the breaking or ruin, of Joseph. Their callousness matches their corruption, for they have turned mishpat, justice, into poison and the fruit of tsedaqah, righteousness, into laanah, wormwood, the same bitter reversal named in chapter five. They boast in Lo-debar, a name that sounds like nothing, and Karnaim, which means horns or strength, congratulating themselves on their own might just as God swears to abhor Jacob's pride and raise up a nation to oppress them.

typology.type: The complacent elite who feast while ruin nears, 6:4-6
typology.fulfillment: Answered by Christ, the King who refused comfort, wept over the doomed city, Luke 19:41, and entered the nation's calamity to bear it.

typology.type: The woe on those secure apart from God, 6:1
typology.fulfillment: The judgment Christ absorbed at the cross, so that those who mourn their sin and hide in Him escape the woe, Matthew 5:4.

typology.type: Exile at the head of the captives, 6:7
typology.fulfillment: The exile of judgment that Christ endured in our place, going outside the camp, Hebrews 13:12, to bring His people home.

study.christ: The rulers of Samaria lounged on ivory beds and would not grieve over Joseph's ruin; Jesus, the true King of Israel, had nowhere to lay His head, wept aloud over Jerusalem as it hurtled toward calamity, and gave up every comfort to enter the ruin and bear it. The hoy, the woe, that Amos pronounces over the complacent is the very sentence Christ took upon Himself at the cross, drinking the cup of judgment down to the dregs. So the door stands open: those who stop dismissing the day of calamity, who mourn their sin rather than feast past it, find in Christ not exile but welcome, and the pride God abhors is answered by the humility of the One who emptied Himself.
study.god: God is scandalized by security that feels no grief. He swears by Himself, the strongest oath possible, that He abhors Jacob's pride and detests the fortified citadels in which the elite trust. He is sovereign over history, able to raise up a whole nation as the rod of His judgment and to march the self-satisfied off first into exile. Yet this same God is not merely a destroyer of the proud; in Christ He enters the very calamity He announces, weeping over the city and bearing its woe, so that His severity toward pride and His mercy toward the humble meet at the cross.

crossReferences.note: Jesus pronounces woe on the rich and full who laugh now, directly echoing the woe of Amos on the complacent.
The rich fool stores up ease for himself and loses his soul, the same fatal complacency Amos exposes.
The self-indulgent rich who fattened themselves and ignored the cries of the poor face coming misery, a New Testament parallel to Amos 6.
Christ weeps over Jerusalem facing judgment, the opposite of the elite who would not grieve over the ruin of Joseph.

study.oneStory: The comfortable feast while the nation dies and refuse to mourn its ruin, so God swears to hand the proud city over. The story turns on Jesus, the King who wept over a doomed city, gave up all comfort, and took the woe of judgment on Himself, so that everyone who mourns sin instead of dismissing the day of calamity is welcomed home rather than led away into exile.

quiz.q: What is the fatal charge Amos brings against the comfortable elite of Samaria?

options.text: They feel completely secure and are not grieved over the ruin of their own nation.
options.note: Verse six names the missing thing: no grief over the ruin of Joseph.

options.text: They own furniture and enjoy food and music.
options.note: Comfort itself is not the crime; callous indifference to ruin is.

options.text: They live in luxury while ignoring the coming collapse.
options.note: Right about the luxury and indifference; the sharpest point is their lack of grief over Joseph's ruin.

options.text: They forgot to attend the religious festivals.
options.note: The issue is not missed worship but a heart at ease and unmoved by ruin.

quiz.q: Why does Amos begin the chapter with woe, the same cry raised at a funeral?

options.text: He pronounces the secure elite as good as dead, turning their false peace into a lament.
options.note: The funeral cry falls on those who feel most safe.

options.text: Because someone in Samaria had actually died.
options.note: The woe is a prophetic verdict, not a report of a literal funeral.

options.text: Because judgment is coming on the complacent.
options.note: True, and the funeral overtone shows they are already reckoned dead in their security.

options.text: Because Amos was in a gloomy mood.
options.note: The woe is a deliberate prophetic form, not a personal mood.

quiz.q: What does God swear about Jacob's pride?

options.text: He abhors their pride and their fortified citadels and will deliver the city over to judgment.
options.note: God takes the strongest oath, by Himself, against their self-exalting pride.

options.text: He is impressed by their strength and secure cities.
options.note: God detests, not admires, their pride and citadels.

options.text: He is displeased with their arrogance.
options.note: Right, and He binds it to a solemn oath to hand the city over.

options.text: He will overlook it because they are His people.
options.note: Being His people does not shield the proud; He swears against them.

quiz.q: How does the complacency Amos condemns point us to Christ?

options.text: Where they lounged and would not grieve, Jesus refused comfort, wept over the doomed city, and bore its woe.
options.note: Christ is the opposite of the callous elite and the One who takes their woe.

options.text: It simply teaches us to give away our furniture.
options.note: A possible application, but it misses that Christ Himself answers the woe.

options.text: It shows that Jesus also warned against greed.
options.note: True that He warned of it, but the deeper link is that He bore the woe for us.

options.text: It has no connection to Jesus.
options.note: Christ weeping over the city and bearing judgment directly answers this passage.

quiz.q: What warning does Amos 6 hold for people who feel spiritually secure today?

options.text: Comfort that leaves us unmoved by sin and suffering, and confident apart from God, is deadly, not safe.
options.note: False security blind to ruin is the very danger Amos exposes.

options.text: That we should never rest or enjoy anything.
options.note: Scripture allows rest; it warns against complacency that deadens compassion.

options.text: That feeling at ease is a sign of God's approval.
options.note: Amos shows ease can be the mask over coming judgment.

options.text: That we should stay alert and tender rather than complacent.
options.note: Right direction; the passage roots this in the danger of security apart from God.

questions: Amos condemns those who felt at ease while their nation was in ruin. Where has comfort made you numb to sin or suffering around you?
The elite were not grieved over the ruin of Joseph. What ruin, in your community or the wider world, has God been calling you to grieve rather than ignore?
They boasted in Lo-debar and Karnaim, in their own strength. Where are you quietly trusting your own achievements instead of God?
God swore that He abhors pride. Is there a citadel of self-confidence in your life that you need to surrender to Him?
Jesus wept over a doomed city rather than feasting past its danger. How does His grief reshape the way you respond to a world under judgment?

translationNotes.note: The finest oils translates a phrase for the choicest ointments; the picture is of the elite pampering themselves with premium luxury even as the nation breaks apart.

The relative who is to burn the bodies may also be rendered the one who makes a funeral fire to honor the dead; the grim scene assumes a plague or slaughter so severe that survivors dare not even invoke the name of the LORD.

Lo-debar sounds like the Hebrew for nothing, and Karnaim means horns, a symbol of strength; Amos mocks Israel for boasting that they took a place called Nothing and a place called Strength by their own power.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for Amos 6:1-14. Poetic line fragments joined into running verse text; footnote markers omitted; em dashes in verses 8 and 12 rendered as commas per house style. Tokens for justice and righteousness reuse the book's established keys at verse 12.
